revision.text:
[[ca:Daijō-kan]][[en:Daijō-kan]][[es:Daijō-kan]][[fa:دایجوکان]][[fi:Dajōkan]][[fr:Daijō-kan]][[id:Daijō-kan]][[it:Daijō-kan]][[ja:太政官]][[ko:태정관]][[pt:Daijō-kan]][[ru:Дайдзёкан]][[simple:Daijō-kan]][[sr:Даиџо-кан]][[th:ไดโจกัง]][[tr:Daijō-kan]][[uk:Дайдзьокан]][[vi:Thái chính quan]][[wuu:太政官]][[zh-classical:太政官]][[zh:太政官]] {{Short description|Highest organ of the imperial Japanese government in the Nara period and Meiji Restoration|qq=}} {{Daijō-kan|qq=}} The {{Nihongo|'''Daijō-kan''' or '''Dajō-kan'''|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|lead=yes|qq=}},''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'', Kenkyusha Limited, {{ISBN|4-7674-2015-6|qq=}} also known as the '''Great [[:en:Council of State|Council of State]]''', was (i) (''Daijō-kan'') the highest organ of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}'s premodern Imperial government under the [[Ritsuryō]] legal system during and after the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} or (ii) (''Dajō-kan'') the highest organ of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}'s government briefly restored to power after the [[Meiji Restoration]], which was replaced by the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}}. It was consolidated in the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} of 702. The {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qid=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} of 689 marks the initial appearance of this central administrative body composed of the three ministers—the ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' (Chancellor), the ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'' (Minister of the Left) and the ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'' (Minister of the Right).Hall, John Whitney ''et al.''. (1993). [https://books.google.com/books?id=nCJwEDzyxNgC&dq=Asuka+code&pg=PA232 ''The Cambridge History of Japan'', p. 232.] The Imperial governing structure was headed by the ''Daijō-kan''. This council and its subsidiary ministries handled all secular administrative affairs of the country, while the ''[[Department of Divinities]]'' or Department of Worship, oversaw all matters regarding [[Shinto|Shintō]] ritual, clergy, and shrines. This structured organization gradually lost power over the 10th and 11th centuries, as the [[Fujiwara clan]], dominating the post of [[:en:Sessho and Kampaku|Imperial regent]], began to dominate the ''Daijō-kan'' as well. It became increasingly common for the regent to hold the post of chancellor or other office simultaneously. By the 12th century, the council was essentially powerless as a separate entity, though it seems clear that the system was never formally dismantled. Over centuries, the ''ritsuryō'' state produced more and more information which was carefully archived; however, over time in the Heian period, ''ritsuryō'' institutions evolved into a political and cultural system without feedback.[http://202.231.40.34/jpub/pdf/jr/IJ1508.pdf Mesheryakov, Alexander (2003). "On the Quantity of Written Data Produced by the Ritsuryō State"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110529220438/http://202.231.40.34/jpub/pdf/jr/IJ1508.pdf |date=2011-05-29 |qq=}}, ''Japan Review'', 15:187–199. By the time of the {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}}, the [[kuge]] aristocracy was joined in common goals by several newly powerful provincial figures from outside Kyoto. Together, this tenuous, undefined coalition of men worked to restore the long latent prestige, persuasive power, and active strengths of a re-invigorated Imperial center. This combination of factors thrust an archaic hierarchy into the center of national attention, but with so many other high-priority matters demanding immediate attention, there was little time or energy to invest in reforming or re-organizing the ''Daijō-kan''. ==Ritsuryō organization and hierarchy== The eighth century [[ritsuryō]] innovations would prove to be remarkably durable and resilient across the span of centuries.[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/courtgovt.shtml Ritsuryō -- court structure and offices], Sheffield. Any exercise of meaningful powers of court officials reached its {{ill|nadir|en|nadir|qid=Q145825|lt=nadir|1=Nadir}} during the years of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], and yet the ''Daijō-kan'' did manage to persist intact through the initial years of the [[Meiji Restoration]].Ozaki, Yukio. (2001). ''The Autobiography of Ozaki Yukio: The Struggle for Constitutional Government in Japan'' pp. 10-11. It is not possible to assess or evaluate any individual office without assessing its role in the context of a durable yet flexible network and hierarchy of functionaries.Dickson, Walter G. ''et al.'' (1898). {{Google books|s9YeAAAAMAAJ|"The Eight Boards of Government" in ''Japan,'' pp. 55-78.|page=56|qq=}}; excerpt at p. 56, "Klaproth has given in his "Annals of the Emperors" a sketch of these eight boards, with the offices under each. It is ... a concise account of the government of Japan. The study of such a subject is rather dry and uninteresting, but it is necessary for any one who wishes to make himself acquainted with Japanese history, either of the past or of the present day...." In the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the appointed Imperial ''Daijo-kan'' was filled with princes, aristocrats, loyalists domain lords (''daimyō''), and samurai.Ozaki, pp. 1-6. Within months after {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}'s {{ill|Charter Oath|en|Charter Oath|qid=Q702056|lt=Charter Oath|1=Charter Oath}}, the ancient ritsuryō structure was slightly modified with an express focus on the separation of legislative, administrative, and judicial functions within the ''Daijō-kan'' system.Ozaki, p. 10. The evolution of a deliberative body within a modern constitutional system was gradual, and its constituent differences from the old ''Daijō-kan'' were not entirely self-evident at first, as revealed in an Imperial message in 1869:
The Assembly shall be for the wide ranging consultation of public opinion and, respecting the Imperial will which laid the foundations of national government, it will be a place where the energies of the multitude are harnessed. Thus, it is necessary that proceedings will show respect for the Imperial rescript, be united in purpose with the ''Daijō-kan'', take the fundamentals of government to heart, judiciously a11ress matters which arise, and act to ensure that unity within the country is not compromised.Ozaki, p. 11.
Some months later, another major reform of the ''Daijō-kan'' re-united the legislative and executive functions which had been clearly separated earlier. In 1871, The office of ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' in the Great Council of State was briefly resurrected under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}} with the appointment of [[Sanjō Sanetomi]]. Despite the similarity of names for its constituent offices, this ''Daijō-kan'' would have been unrecognizable to Fujiwara courtiers of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. Nor would it have seemed at all familiar to those men who surrounded the emperor in the days of the [[:en:Kenmu restoration|Kenmu restoration]]. In due course, it was decided that a modern integrated cabinet system would better serve a modern Japan. The ''Daijō-kan'' system, which had been divided into ministerial committees, would be replaced by a more modern model. In December 1885, the old system was abolished completely;Ozaki, p. 86. and yet, even afterwards, some elements of old system were adapted to new uses. For example, in that year, the title of ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'' was reconfigured to mean the {{ill|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|en|Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|qid=Q712143|lt=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan|1=Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal of Japan}} in the [[:en:Court (royal)|Imperial Court]].Dus, Peter. (1988). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: The Twentieth Century,'' pp. 59, 81. The man who had previously held the office of prime minister or chief minister of the initial restoration government was the ''Daijō-daijin'', [[Sanjō Sanetomi]]. Sanjō petitioned the emperor to be relieved of his ancient ritsuryō office; and he was then immediately appointed ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'', or Lord Keeper of the Privy Seal. The office of the Privy Seal was identical with the old ''Naidaijin'' only in its Japanese title—not in terms of function or powers.[http://www.unterstein.net/ Unterstein (in German)]: [http://www.unterstein.net/or/docs/JapanPeers.pdf Ranks in Ancient and Meiji Japan (in English and French)], pp. 6, 27. ==Council of State== This dry catalog does provide a superficial glimpse inside the complexity of what was initially designed as a pre-feudal court structure. What this list cannot easily explain is how or why the ''Daijō-kan'' turned out to be both flexible and useful across a span of centuries: * {{nihongo|[[:en:Daijō Daijin|Chancellor of the Realm]]|太政大臣|{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}|qq=}}Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 425.|page=425|qq=}} See also, {{nihongo|Acting Chancellor|知太政官事|Chi-daijōkanji|qq=}}.Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p.272. * {{nihongo|[[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]]|左大臣|1=Minister of the Left|[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|[[:en:Udaijin|Minister of the Right]]|右大臣|[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Minister of the Center|1=Naidaijin}}|内大臣|{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|lt=Major Counselor|1=Dainagon}}|大納言|{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}|qq=}}, three positions. There are commonly three ''Dainagon'', sometimes more. * {{nihongo|{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|qid=Q1185382|lt=Mi11le Counselor|1=Chūnagon}}|中納言|{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|qid=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon|1=Chūnagon}}|qq=}}, three positions. There are commonly three ''Chunagon'', sometimes more. * {{nihongo|{{ill|Shōnagon|en|Shōnagon|qid=Q859667|lt=Minor Counselor|1=Shōnagon}}|少納言|{{ill|Shōnagon|en|Shōnagon|qid=Q859667|lt=Shōnagon|1=Shōnagon}}|qq=}}, three positions. There are commonly three ''Shōnagon''. * {{nihongo|{{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor|1=Sangi (Japan)}}|参議|1=Sangi (Japan)|lt=Sangi|2=ja|3=参議|4=ru|5=Санги|6=fr|7=Sangi_(Japon)|8=ko|9=참의_(일본사)|10=es|11=Sangi|{{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=Sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}}|qq=}}.Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Sangi" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 817|page=817|qq=}}. This office functions as a manager of daijō-kan activities within the palace.Titsingh, {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|p. 426.|page=426|qq=}} * {{nihongo|External secretariat|外記|Geki|qq=}}. These are specifically named men who act at the sole discretion of the emperor. * {{nihongo|Major controller of the left|左大弁|Sadaiben|qq=}}. This administrator was charged or tasked with supervising four ministries: Center, Civil Services, Ceremonies, and Popular Affairs. * {{nihongo|Major controller of the right|右大弁|Udaiben|qq=}} This administrator was charged or tasked with supervising four ministries: Military, Justice, Treasury and Imperial Household. * {{nihongo|First assistant controller of the left|左中弁|Sachūben|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|First assistant controller of the right|右中弁|Uchūben|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant controller of the left|左少弁|Sashōben|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant controller of the right|右少弁|Ushōben|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|First secretary of the left|左大史|Sadaishi|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|First secretary of the right|右大史|Udaishi|qq=}}. * {{nihongo|Assistant secretaries of the left or right|史生|Shishō|qq=}}, 20 positions. There are twenty officials with this title. ==Ritsuryō Eight Ministries== The {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka period}}-, {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}}- and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}}-period Imperial court hierarchy encompassed a multi-faceted bureaucracy focused on serving the needs of the Emperor, the Imperial family, the Imperial household and the Imperial state.[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/emphoumin.shtml Ministry of Emperor's Household], Sheffield. Imperial power and prestige would wax and wane during the subsequent {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura period}}-, [[:en:Kenmu restoration|Kenmu]]-, {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi|1=Muromachi period}}-, {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō|1=Nanboku-chō period}}-, {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku|1=Sengoku period}}-, {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}-, and {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo|1=Edo period}}-periods; nevertheless, the basic structure of the Imperial household remained largely unchanged. A mere list of the court titles cannot reveal nearly enough about the actual functioning of the ''Daijō-kan''; but the hierarchical relationships sketch a general context. ===Ministry of the Center=== {{Main|Ministry of the Center|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{nihongo|Ministry of the Center|中務省|1=Ministry of the Center|Nakatsukasa-shō|qq=}}, sometimes identified as the "Ministry of Central Affairs."[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/centmin.shtml Ministry of Central Affairs] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110524225031/http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/centmin.shtml |date=2011-05-24 |qq=}}, Sheffield. This ministry became the governmental agency for matters most closely pertaining to the emperor. In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' official within this subdivision of the ''daijō-kan'' was the {{nihongo|chief administrator of the Ministry of the Center|中務卿|Nakatsukasa-kyō|qq=}}. This official had the responsibility to oversee the inspection of the interior apartments of the palace; and he was granted the privilege of retaining his swords in the presence of the emperor.Titsingh, p. 427. Considered central were the {{nihongo|[[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|Emperor's equerries]]|侍従,|[[:en:jijū|jijū]]|qq=}}, 8 positions. There are 8 officials with this title, all equal in Rank and in the confidence of the Emperor. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, a variant equerry was introduced as part of the Imperial retinue. As explained in an excerpt from the {{nihongo|113th Imperial decree of 1896 (''Meiji 29'')|明治29th year 勅令第113号|qq=}}: "{{nihongo|{{ill|Aide-de-camp to the Emperor of Japan|en|Aide-de-camp to the Emperor of Japan|qid=Q704416|lt=Aides-de-camp to the Emperor of Japan|1=Aide-de-camp to the Emperor of Japan}}|侍従武官|[[:en:jijū|jijū bukan]]|qq=}} will perform attendant duties and will relay to him military matters and orders, be present at military reviews [in his name] and accompanying him to formal ceremonies and interviews."侍従武官ハ天皇ニ常侍奉仕シ軍事ニ関スル奏上奉答及命令ノ伝達ニ任シ観兵演習行幸其他祭儀礼典宴会謁見等ニ陪侍扈従ス ===Ministry of Civil Services=== {{Main|Shikibu-sho|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{nihongo|Ministry of the Civil Services|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|Shikibu-shō|qq=}}; also known as the "Ministry of Legislative Direction and Public Instruction".[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/civadminmin.shtml Ministry of Civil Administration] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207171103/http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/civadminmin.shtml |date=2012-02-07 |qq=}}, Sheffield. This ministry collected and maintained biographical archives of meritorious subjects.Ury, Marian. (1999). "Chinese Learning and Intellectual Life," ''The Cambridge history of Japan: Heian Japan'', p. 361. In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' official within this subdivision of the ''daijō-kan'' was the {{nihongo|chief administrator of the Ministry of Civil Services|式部卿|Shikibu-kyō|qq=}};Titsingh, p. 428. also known as "Chief minister of public instruction." This office is ordinarily filled by a son or close relative of the emperor. Two of the offices which were deemed to fit in this "civil services" context were the Imperial court's {{nihongo|Chief judge|式部大輔|Shikibu-taifu|qq=}}. and the Emperor's {{nihongo|{{ill|Daigaku-no-kami|en|Daigaku-no-kami|qid=Q3012043|lt=chief education expert|1=Daigaku-no-kami}}|大学頭|[[:en:Daigaku no kami|Daigaku no kami]]|qq=}}. ===Ministry of Ceremonies=== {{Main|Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{nihongo|Ministry of Ceremonies|治部省|1=Ministry of Ceremonies (Japan)|lt=Ministry of Ceremonies|Jibu-shō|qq=}}; also known as the "Ministry of the Interior".[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/ceremmin.shtml Ministry of Ceremonies] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110524225044/http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/ceremmin.shtml |date=2011-05-24 |qq=}}, Sheffield. In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' official within this subdivision of the ''daijō-kan'' was the {{nihongo|[[:en:Master of Ceremonies (Japan)|chief administrator of the Ministry of Ceremonies]]|治部卿,|[[:en:Jibu-kyō|Jibu-kyō]]|qq=}}. ===Ministry of Popular Affairs=== {{Main|Ministry of Popular Affairs|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{nihongo|Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部省|1=Ministry of Popular Affairs|Minbu-shō|qq=}}.[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/popaffmin.shtml Ministry of Popular Affairs] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727092428/http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/popaffmin.shtml |date=2011-07-27 |qq=}}, Sheffield. This ministry is concerned with the general populace, with police activities, and with land survey records. Registries for all towns and villages are maintained, including census records as well as birth and death records.Titsingh, p. 430. In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' official within this subdivision of the ''daijō-kan'' was the {{nihongo| chief administrator of the Ministry of Popular Affairs|民部卿|Minbu-kyō|qq=}}. ===Ministry of War=== {{Main|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{nihongo|Ministry of War|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|Hyōbu-shō|qq=}}.Titsingh, p. 431. In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' official within this subdivision of the ''daijō-kan'' was the {{nihongo|chief administrator of the Ministry of War|兵部卿|Hyōbu-kyō|qq=}}. ===Ministry of Justice=== {{Main|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{nihongo|Ministry of Justice|刑部省|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|Gyōbu-shō|qq=}}.[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/justmin.shtml Ministry of Justice]{{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727091622/http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/justmin.shtml |archive-date=27 July 2011|qq=}}, Sheffield. In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' official within this subdivision of the ''daijō-kan'' was the {{nihongo|chief administrator of the ministry of justice|刑部卿|Gyōbu-kyō|qq=}}. ===Ministry of the Treasury=== {{Main|Ministry of the Treasury|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{nihongo|Ministry of the Treasury|大蔵省|Ōkura-shō|qq=}}.[http://www.temcauley.staff.shef.ac.uk/tremin.shtml Ministry of the Treasury], Sheffield. In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' official within this subdivision of the ''daijō-kan'' was the {{nihongo|chief administrator of the ministry of the treasury|大蔵卿|Ōkura-kyō|qq=}}. This official supervises the receipt of tributes from the provinces and imposes tribute on others.Titsingh, p. 432. ===Ministry of the Imperial Household=== {{Main|Ministry of the Imperial Household|qq=}} The Taihō Code established a {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|qid=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}}|宮内省|Kunai-shō|qq=}}. The origins of the current {{Nihongo|{{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}|宮内庁|Kunai-chō|qq=}} can be traced back to structures which were put into effect during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|qid=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}},[http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e15/ed15-01.html History of Imperial Household Agency] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071215123901/http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e15/ed15-01.html |date=2007-12-15 |qq=}} with some subsequent modifications. In 1702, the Taika era name for the palace organization, ''kunai-kan'' or "government" of the palace, was changed to the ''kunai-shō'' or "ministry" of the palace. Accompanying this modification, the chief administrative official was afterwards called ''kunai-kyō''. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the ''kunai-shō'' name remained unchanged. There were two other periods of modification and in 1889.[http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/08297a.htm "Ministry of the Imperial Household"], ''Catholic Encyclopedia.'' In the 18th century, the top ''ritsuryō'' officials within this hierarchic structure were: * {{nihongo|Chief administrator of the Imperial household|宮内卿|Kunai-kyō|qq=}}, the surveyor of all works which are executed within the interior of the palace. * {{nihongo|First assistant to the chief of the imperial Household|宮内大輔|Kunai-taifu|qq=}}.Titsingh, p. 433 * {{nihongo|Second assistant to the chief of the imperial household|宮内少輔|Kunai-shō|qq=}}, two persons. * {{nihongo|Third assistant to the chief of the imperial household|宮内丞|Kunai-no-jō|qq=}}, two persons. * {{nihongo|Alternate assistants to the chief of the imperial household|宮内録|Kunai-no-sakan|qq=}}, two persons. The deliberate redundancies at the top were common features of each position in this stable hierarchic [[:en:Model (abstract)|schema]]. Many positions typically mirrored the ''-kyō,'' ''-taifu,'' ''-shō,'' ''-jō,'' and ''-sakan'' pattern.Titsingh, pp. 425-435. ==Tokugawa courtiers== Even nominal administrative powers of court officials reached a nadir during the years of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In this impoverished period, titles and court Rank were still prized by those outside the traditional ''kuge''. The Tokugawa shōguns did not demur when the Emperor offered Rank and an office in the court: * {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} had the Rank of ''Jūichii'' (First Rank, Second Class) and the office of ''Udaijin'' (Great Minister of the Right, i.e., inferior to the Minister of the Left)Screech, T. (2006). ''Secret Memoirs of the Shoguns: Isaac Titsingh and Japan, 1779–1822,'' pp. 157. [''Error in published text was corrected: ''Udaijin'' is Minister of the Right -- not Left.''] * {{ill|Tokugawa Hidetada|en|Tokugawa Hidetada|qid=Q314464|lt=Tokugawa Hidetada|1=Tokugawa Hidetada}} had the Rank of ''Jūichii'' and was ''Daijō Daijin'' (the Great Minister or Chancellor of the Realm)Screech, pp. 157. * {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|qid=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} had the same Rank and was ''Sadaijin'' (Great Minister of the Left, i.e., superior to the Minister of the Right)Screech, pp. 157. [''Error in published text was corrected: ''Sadaijin'' is Minister of the Left - not Right.''] * {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|qid=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna|1=Tokugawa Ietsuna}} had the Rank of ''Shōnii'' (Second Rank, First Class) and was ''Udaijin'' * [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] had the Rank of ''Shōnii'' and was ''Udaijin'' * {{ill|Tokugawa Ienobu|en|Tokugawa Ienobu|qid=Q366734|lt=Tokugawa Ienobu|1=Tokugawa Ienobu}} had the Rank of ''Shōnii'' was ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|qid=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'' (Great Minister of the center, i.e., inferior to the Minister of the Left)Screech, pp. 157. [''Error in published text was corrected: ''Nadaijin'' is Minister of the Left - not Right.'']. * {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|qid=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu|1=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} had the Rank of ''Shōnii'' was also ''Nadaijin'' * {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|qid=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune|1=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} held the Rank of ''Shōnii'' and was ''Udaijin'' * {{ill|Tokugawa Ieshige|en|Tokugawa Ieshige|qid=Q323443|lt=Tokugawa Ieshige|1=Tokugawa Ieshige}} also held the Rank of ''Shōnii'' and was ''Udaijin'' * {{ill|Tokugawa Ieharu|en|Tokugawa Ieharu|qid=Q437518|lt=Tokugawa Ieharu|1=Tokugawa Ieharu}} also held the Rank of ''Shōnii'' and was ''Udaijin'' * {{ill|Tokugawa Ienari|en|Tokugawa Ienari|qid=Q439717|lt=Tokugawa Ienari|1=Tokugawa Ienari}} was ''Dainagon'' (Great Counselor) * {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyoshi|en|Tokugawa Ieyoshi|qid=Q439675|lt=Tokugawa Ieyoshi|1=Tokugawa Ieyoshi}} * {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|qid=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada|1=Tokugawa Iesada}} * [[Tokugawa Iemochi]] * [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] ==Geo-political sub-divisions== The country was divided into provinces called {{Nihongo||国|{{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|qid=Q860290|lt=kuni|1=Provinces of Japan}}|qq=}}, which were administered by governors {{Nihongo||国司|{{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}}|qq=}} appointed by the ''Daijō-kan''. The provinces were then further divided into districts called {{Nihongo||郡|gun|/kōri|qq=}}, under district governors {{Nihongo||郡司|gunji|qq=}} who were appointed by the local nobility. At the beginning of the eighth century, there were 592 districts making up 66 provinces. ==See also== * [[Engishiki]] * {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} * {{ill|Kōkyū|en|Kōkyū|qid=Q13403578|lt=Kōkyū|1=Kōkyū}} * [[Kuge]] * {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|qid=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}} * [[Sesshō and Kampaku]] * {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} * {{ill|Takahashi Ujibumi|en|Takahashi Ujibumi|qid=Q1108567|lt=Takahashi Ujibumi|1=Takahashi Ujibumi}} * [[Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] * {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} ==Notes== {{Reflist|2|qq=}} ==References== {{Refbegin|qq=}} * Asai T. New York: {{ill|Columbia University Press|en|Columbia University Press|qid=Q2080179|lt=Columbia University Press|1=Columbia University Press}}. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4|qq=}} {{Refend|qq=}} ==External links== *{{ill|National Archives of Japan|en|National Archives of Japan|qid=Q1072875|lt=National Archives of Japan|1=National Archives of Japan}} – [https://web.archive.org/web/20091124223638/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/drawing/dajoukanshinchikuzu_e.html Drawings of the Dajokan Building (November, 1877)] * National Archives of Japan – [https://web.archive.org/web/20091124223633/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/drawing/dajoukanshinchikuezu_e.html Illustration of the new Dajokan Building (1877)] * National Archives of Japan – [https://web.archive.org/web/20091124223711/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/drawing/naimusyo_e.html Drawings for construction of the new building housing the ministries of Home Affairs and Finance (1874)] * National Archives of Japan – [https://web.archive.org/web/20080208213302/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/drawing/koseki_e.html Request concerning family registration statistics (1869)] {{JapanEmpireNavbox|qq=}} [[en:Daijō-kan]] [[ca:Daijō-kan]] [[es:Daijō-kan]] [[fa:دایجوکان]] [[fi:Dajōkan]] [[fr:Daijō-kan]] [[id:Daijō-kan]] [[it:Daijō-kan]] [[ja:太政官]] [[ko:태정관]] [[pt:Daijō-kan]] [[ru:Дайдзёкан]] [[simple:Daijō-kan]] [[sr:Даиџо-кан]] [[th:ไดโจกัง]] [[tr:Daijō-kan]] [[uk:Дайдзьокан]] [[vi:Thái chính quan]] [[wuu:太政官]] [[zh:太政官]] [[zh-classical:太政官]] {{wikidata link|Q1192939}} [[Category:Classical Japan]] [[Category:Former government ministries of Japan]] [[Category:Government of feudal Japan]] [[Category:Meiji Restoration]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Officials of the Ritsuryō System]] [[Category:日本の律令制]]
